column1: 6 J/cm2 and 12 J/cm2 to evaluate the phototoxic effect. Laser-treated cells were further incubated for 24 h to
determine relative cell viability using the MTT assay34.
Reactive oxygen species (ROS) analysis
ROS levels were detected with 2′,7′-Dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFDA) reagent. After laser-treated cells, 30
μL of DCFDA (10 μM) was added, and then the cells were kept for 40 min at 37 °C under dark conditions. A
fluorescence microscope was used to visualize the cells37.
Morphological study
The  inverted  visible  light  microscope  (Optika  Microscope,  Italy,  XDS-2 + M795)  was  used  to  investigate  the
morphological changes of treated and untreated BT-474 cells25. Uncropped histology images of each examination
are included in Supplementary File 2 (Figures S1, S2, S3 and S4).
Statistical analysis
All tests were analyzed in triplicate, and Microsoft Excel and Origin Pro 2023 were used to generate the charts
and graphs. All data from 3 independent experiments are shown as the mean ± standard deviation. The mean
values  of  the  treatments  were  compared  using  the  student  t-test,  where  p < 0.05  was  considered  statistically
significant.
Results
Phytochemical screening
FI extract possesses various bioactive components. Qualitative phytochemical analysis confirmed the presence
of flavonoids, alkaloids, phenols, glycosides, proteins, and saponins in the plant extract (Supplementary file 1,
Table S2).
Drug solubility study
The drug solubility testing of 10 mg of FI extract and synthesized FI-PLGA NPs in distilled water is shown in
Fig. 1. FI-PLGA NPs exhibited a homogenous solution (Fig. 1 b), whereas the FI extract was not completely
soluble in distilled water as small particles were left undissolved (Fig. 1 a), which indicated that drug solubility
was improved when FI extract was encapsulated inside the PLGA NPs.
Characterization of nanoparticles
The nanocarrier’s size is a key factor that affects the treatment efficacy. The hydrodynamic size of prepared NPs
was  measured  using  dynamic  light  scattering  (Fig.  2).  Blank  PLGA  NPs  displayed  a  particle  size  of  192  nm,
PDI 0.087, and a negative charge of -21.7 mV. The nanoparticles exhibited a negative zeta potential, which is
attributed to the terminal carboxylic groups present in the polymer. The particle size, PDI, and zeta potential of
FI-PLGA NPs were measured as 217 nm, 0.093, and -30.5 mV, respectively. The increase in particle size, PDI, and
zeta potential attributed to FI extract successfully encapsulated into PLGA NPs.
The  encapsulation  efficiency  determines  the  specific  quantity  of  drug  or  compound  that  is  incorporated
within the PLGA matrix. The amount of free FI extract was determined by measuring the absorbance at 272 nm,
